user_text: generate a complete lesson that shows a beginner what a "dictionary" is in python. i need a runable python file. some quiz examples and basic textual explainations

messages.content:
You are an expert autonomous Task Orchestrator.
Your goal is to analyze user requests and educational materials, then create a structured execution plan.

Available Agents:
- 'coder': Generates runnable Python code. Best for demonstrations, data analysis, and coding tutorials.
- 'video': Creates video content. Best for visual explanations.
- 'text': Generates markdown text/notes. Best for summaries, lesson plans, and articles.
- 'quizzer': Creates quizzes. Best for assessments and review.

Instructions:
1. Analyze the user's input and provided files.
2. Break down the goal into logical subtasks.
3. Assign each subtask to the most appropriate agent.
4. Provide specific, actionable instructions for each agent.
5. For every subtask, include a non-empty 'acceptance_criteria' list.
   - Each criterion must include: criterion_id, criterion_type, target, operator, expected, severity.
   - Use criterion_type values: mcp_tool | output_shape | file_exists | semantic.
   - Use operator values: equals | contains | exists | gte | lte.
   - Use target paths that start with 'result.' or 'tools.'.
   - For mcp_tool criteria, include tool: { name, args }.
   - Use placeholders like $artifacts[0] or $output.validation.path inside tool args.
6. Use the provided tool 'create_task_plan' to submit your plan.

Response Format Example:
{
  "user_intent": "Create a lesson on Python lists with a video intro and a quiz.",
  "subtasks": [
    {
      "task_id": "step1_code",
      "agent": "coder",
      "instruction": "Generate a Python script demonstrating list operations.",
      "inputs": {},
      "dependencies": [],
      "acceptance_criteria": [
        {
          "criterion_id": "code_executes",
          "criterion_type": "mcp_tool",
          "tool": {"name": "python_check", "args": {"filepath": "$artifacts[0]"}},
          "target": "tools.python_check.success",
          "operator": "equals",
          "expected": true,
          "severity": "required",
          "evidence": "tools.python_check.run.stderr"
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "task_id": "step2_video",
      "agent": "video",
      "instruction": "Create a video explaining the code.",
      "inputs": {},
      "dependencies": ["step1_code"],
      "acceptance_criteria": [
        {
          "criterion_id": "video_semantic_ok",
          "criterion_type": "semantic",
          "target": "result.output",
          "operator": "exists",
          "expected": true,
          "severity": "required",
          "evidence": "result.output"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}

content.text: generate a complete lesson that shows a beginner what a "dictionary" is in python. i need a runable python file. some quiz examples and basic textual explainations

function.name: create_task_plan
function.description: Create a structured execution plan for multiple agents.

type.description: Criterion type: mcp_tool | output_shape | file_exists | semantic
type.title: Type

target.description: Evaluation target path (e.g., result.output.text or tools.python_check.success)
target.title: Target

operator.description: Operator: equals | contains | exists | gte | lte
operator.title: Operator

tool.description: Tool call definition for mcp_tool criteria
tool.title: Tool

evidence.description: Evidence path to include in judger output
evidence.title: Evidence

agent.description: The agent responsible (coder, video, text, quizzer)
agent.title: Agent

dependencies.description: IDs of tasks that must complete first
dependencies.title: Dependencies

acceptance_criteria.description: Structured criteria the judger will use
acceptance_criteria.title: Acceptance Criteria

user_intent.description: Summary of what the user wants to achieve
user_intent.title: User Intent

tool_calls.name: create_task_plan
tool_calls.arguments: {"user_intent":"Create a complete beginner-friendly lesson on Python dictionaries, including a runnable Python file, basic textual explanations, and quiz examples.","subtasks":[{"id":"text_lesson","agent":"text","title":"Write a concise beginner lesson in Markdown explaining what a Python dictionary is, core operations (create, read, update, delete), common methods (keys, values, items, get), iteration patterns, nesting, and typical use cases. Include short code snippets and best practices (key immutability, .get vs [], membership with in).","dependencies":[],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"lesson_exists","type":"output_shape","target":"result.output.markdown","operator":"exists","expected":true,"severity":"required","evidence":"result.output.markdown"},{"id":"lesson_mentions_core_ops","type":"semantic","target":"result.output.markdown","operator":"contains","expected":"create, read, update, delete","severity":"optional","evidence":"result.output.markdown"},{"id":"lesson_includes_code_blocks","type":"semantic","target":"result.output.markdown","operator":"contains","expected":"```python","severity":"required","evidence":"result.output.markdown"}]},{"id":"coder_runnable_file","agent":"coder","title":"Create a runnable Python script (e.g., dictionary_lesson.py) that demonstrates dictionary basics step-by-step with printed headings. Must cover: creating dicts, accessing values, adding/updating, deleting with del and pop, using get with default, iterating keys/values/items, checking membership, and a small mini-exercise (e.g., word frequency counter or simple phonebook). Include a main guard and make sure it runs without external dependencies.","dependencies":["text_lesson"],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"file_written","type":"file_exists","target":"result.artifacts[0].path","operator":"exists","expected":true,"severity":"required","evidence":"result.artifacts[0].path"},{"id":"script_has_main_guard","type":"semantic","target":"result.artifacts[0].content","operator":"contains","expected":"if __name__ == \"__main__\":","severity":"required","evidence":"result.artifacts[0].content"},{"id":"covers_dict_methods","type":"semantic","target":"result.artifacts[0].content","operator":"contains","expected":".items()","severity":"required","evidence":"result.artifacts[0].content"},{"id":"covers_get_default","type":"semantic","target":"result.artifacts[0].content","operator":"contains","expected":".get(","severity":"required","evidence":"result.artifacts[0].content"}]},{"id":"quiz_examples","agent":"quizzer","title":"Create a short quiz section (10–12 questions) for beginners about dictionaries. Mix multiple choice, true/false, and a few small coding prompts. Provide an answer key with brief explanations.","dependencies":["text_lesson"],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"quiz_has_10_plus","type":"semantic","target":"result.output.quiz","operator":"contains","expected":"Answer Key","severity":"required","evidence":"result.output.quiz"},{"id":"quiz_mentions_get_vs_brackets","type":"semantic","target":"result.output.quiz","operator":"contains","expected":".get","severity":"optional","evidence":"result.output.quiz"},{"id":"quiz_includes_coding_prompt","type":"semantic","target":"result.output.quiz","operator":"contains","expected":"Write","severity":"optional","evidence":"result.output.quiz"}]},{"id":"bundle_delivery","agent":"text","title":"Assemble the final deliverable: (1) the markdown lesson, (2) link/instructions to run the Python script, and (3) the quiz + answer key. Ensure consistency between examples and the runnable file name.","dependencies":["coder_runnable_file","quiz_examples","text_lesson"],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"final_has_run_instructions","type":"semantic","target":"result.output.final","operator":"contains","expected":"python","severity":"required","evidence":"result.output.final"},{"id":"final_mentions_script_name","type":"semantic","target":"result.output.final","operator":"contains","expected":".py","severity":"required","evidence":"result.output.final"},{"id":"final_includes_quiz","type":"semantic","target":"result.output.final","operator":"contains","expected":"Answer Key","severity":"required","evidence":"result.output.final"}]}]}

parsed_plan.user_intent: Create a complete beginner-friendly lesson on Python dictionaries, including a runnable Python file, basic textual explanations, and quiz examples.

subtasks.instruction: Write a concise beginner lesson in Markdown explaining what a Python dictionary is, core operations (create, read, update, delete), common methods (keys, values, items, get), iteration patterns, nesting, and typical use cases. Include short code snippets and best practices (key immutability, .get vs [], membership with in).

Create a runnable Python script (e.g., dictionary_lesson.py) that demonstrates dictionary basics step-by-step with printed headings. Must cover: creating dicts, accessing values, adding/updating, deleting with del and pop, using get with default, iterating keys/values/items, checking membership, and a small mini-exercise (e.g., word frequency counter or simple phonebook). Include a main guard and make sure it runs without external dependencies.
Create a short quiz section (10–12 questions) for beginners about dictionaries. Mix multiple choice, true/false, and a few small coding prompts. Provide an answer key with brief explanations.
Assemble the final deliverable: (1) the markdown lesson, (2) link/instructions to run the Python script, and (3) the quiz + answer key. Ensure consistency between examples and the runnable file name.

final_plan.user_intent: Create a complete beginner-friendly lesson on Python dictionaries, including a runnable Python file, basic textual explanations, and quiz examples.

subtasks.instruction: Write a concise beginner lesson in Markdown explaining what a Python dictionary is, core operations (create, read, update, delete), common methods (keys, values, items, get), iteration patterns, nesting, and typical use cases. Include short code snippets and best practices (key immutability, .get vs [], membership with in).

Create a runnable Python script (e.g., dictionary_lesson.py) that demonstrates dictionary basics step-by-step with printed headings. Must cover: creating dicts, accessing values, adding/updating, deleting with del and pop, using get with default, iterating keys/values/items, checking membership, and a small mini-exercise (e.g., word frequency counter or simple phonebook). Include a main guard and make sure it runs without external dependencies.
Create a short quiz section (10–12 questions) for beginners about dictionaries. Mix multiple choice, true/false, and a few small coding prompts. Provide an answer key with brief explanations.
Assemble the final deliverable: (1) the markdown lesson, (2) link/instructions to run the Python script, and (3) the quiz + answer key. Ensure consistency between examples and the runnable file name.

output.text: Generated text content based on: Produce the actual Markdown lesson content at result.output.markdown (not placeholder text). Include at least one fenced Python code block starting with ```python. Explicitly mention and demonstrate CRUD operations (create, read, update, delete). Cover methods keys(), values(), items(), get(), iteration patterns, nesting, typical use cases, and best practices: keys must be immutable, prefer .get for missing keys vs [], and membership testing with `in`.

text_lesson.instruction: Produce the actual Markdown lesson content at result.output.markdown (not placeholder text). Include at least one fenced Python code block starting with ```python. Explicitly mention and demonstrate CRUD operations (create, read, update, delete). Cover methods keys(), values(), items(), get(), iteration patterns, nesting, typical use cases, and best practices: keys must be immutable, prefer .get for missing keys vs [], and membership testing with `in`.

quiz_examples.instruction: The submission is incomplete: it only includes 2 questions and does not provide a 10–12 question beginner dictionaries quiz with a mixed format and an Answer Key section with brief explanations. Update the output to include 10–12 questions (mix multiple choice, true/false, and several short coding prompts) and add an explicit 'Answer Key' with brief explanations for each question. Including a question contrasting dict key access via brackets vs .get and at least one prompt starting with 'Write …' is recommended to satisfy optional criteria.

output.code:
def main():
    print("=== Dictionary Basics Lesson ===\n")
    
    # 1. Creating dictionaries
    print("1. Creating Dictionaries")
    print("-" * 20)
    empty_dict = {}
    person = {"name": "Alice", "age": 30, "city": "New York"}
    print(f"Empty dictionary: {empty_dict}")
    print(f"Person dictionary: {person}\n")
    
    # 2. Accessing values
    print("2. Accessing Values")
    print("-" * 20)
    print(f"Name: {person['name']}")
    print(f"Age: {person['age']}")
    # Using get() method for safe access
    print(f"Occupation (using get): {person.get('occupation', 'Not specified')}")
    print()
    
    # 3. Adding and updating values
    print("3. Adding and Updating Values")
    print("-" * 20)
    person["email"] = "alice@example.com"  # Adding new key-value pair
    person["age"] = 31  # Updating existing value
    print(f"After update: {person}")
    print()
    
    # 4. Deleting items
    print("4. Deleting Items")
    print("-" * 20)
    del person["city"]  # Delete using del
    email = person.pop("email")  # Delete using pop
    print(f"Deleted email: {email}")
    print(f"After deletion: {person}")
    print()
    
    # 5. Using get() method
    print("5. Using get() Method")
    print("-" * 20)
    # Safe access with default values
    phone = person.get("phone", "No phone number")
    print(f"Phone number: {phone}")
    print()
    
    # 6. Iterating through dictionaries
    print("6. Iterating Through Dictionaries")
    print("-" * 20)
    print("Keys:")
    for key in person:
        print(f"  {key}")
    
    print("\nValues:")
    for value in person.values():
        print(f"  {value}")
    
    print("\nKey-Value pairs:")
    for key, value in person.items():
        print(f"  {key}: {value}")
    print()
    
    # 7. Membership checks
    print("7. Membership Checks")
    print("-" * 20)
    print(f"Is 'name' in person? {'name' in person}")
    print(f"Is 'city' in person? {'city' in person}")
    print(f"Is 'Alice' in person? {'Alice' in person}")  # Checks keys only
    print()
    
    # 8. Mini Exercise: Word Frequency Counter
    print("8. Mini Exercise: Word Frequency Counter")
    print("-" * 20)
    text = "the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog the fox was quick"
    words = text.split()
    
    # Count word frequencies
    word_count = {}
    for word in words:
        word_count[word] = word_count.get(word, 0) + 1
    
    print(f"Text: {text}")
    print("Word frequencies:")
    for word, count in word_count.items():
        print(f"  {word}: {count}")
    print()
    
    # 9. Mini Exercise: Simple Phonebook
    print("9. Mini Exercise: Simple Phonebook")
    print("-" * 20)
    phonebook = {
        "Alice": "555-1234",
        "Bob": "555-5678",
        "Charlie": "555-9012"
    }
    
    print("Initial phonebook:")
    for name, phone in phonebook.items():
        print(f"  {name}: {phone}")
    
    # Add a new contact
    phonebook["Diana"] = "555-3456"
    print("\nAfter adding Diana:")
    for name, phone in phonebook.items():
        print(f"  {name}: {phone}")
    
    # Look up a contact
    search_name = "Bob"
    phone = phonebook.get(search_name, "Contact not found")
    print(f"\nPhone number for {search_name}: {phone}")
    
    # Remove a contact
    removed = phonebook.pop("Alice", "Not found")
    print(f"\nRemoved {removed} from phonebook")
    print("Updated phonebook:")
    for name, phone in phonebook.items():
        print(f"  {name}: {phone}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

=== Dictionary Basics Lesson ===

1. Creating Dictionaries
--------------------
Empty dictionary: {}
Person dictionary: {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 30, 'city': 'New York'}

2. Accessing Values
--------------------
Name: Alice
Age: 30
Occupation (using get): Not specified

3. Adding and Updating Values
--------------------
After update: {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 31, 'city': 'New York', 'email': 'alice@example.com'}

4. Deleting Items
--------------------
Deleted email: alice@example.com
After deletion: {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 31}

5. Using get() Method
--------------------
Phone number: No phone number

6. Iterating Through Dictionaries
--------------------
Keys:
  name
  age

Values:
  Alice
  31

Key-Value pairs:
  name: Alice
  age: 31

7. Membership Checks
--------------------
Is 'name' in person? True
Is 'city' in person? False
Is 'Alice' in person? False

8. Mini Exercise: Word Frequency Counter
--------------------
Text: the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog the fox was quick
Word frequencies:
  the: 3
  quick: 2
  brown: 1
  fox: 2
  jumps: 1
  over: 1
  lazy: 1
  dog: 1
  was: 1

9. Mini Exercise: Simple Phonebook
--------------------
Initial phonebook:
  Alice: 555-1234
  Bob: 555-5678
  Charlie: 555-9012

After adding Diana:
  Alice: 555-1234
  Bob: 555-5678
  Charlie: 555-9012
  Diana: 555-3456

Phone number for Bob: 555-5678

Removed 555-1234 from phonebook
Updated phonebook:
  Bob: 555-5678
  Charlie: 555-9012
  Diana: 555-3456

coder_runnable_file.instruction: The produced script prints “Hello, World!” and does not demonstrate dictionary basics. Create a single runnable Python file (e.g., dictionary_lesson.py) that step-by-step prints headings and includes: creating dicts, accessing values, adding/updating, deleting with del and pop, using get(key, default), iterating keys/values/items, membership checks (in), and a small mini-exercise (e.g., word frequency counter or phonebook). Include a main() and an if __name__ == "__main__": guard. Ensure the output artifact is the actual lesson script (not a script that generates another file) and that the file_written indicator is true/available.

output.text: Generated text content based on: Replace the placeholder deliverable with a complete markdown package containing: (1) a full lesson in Markdown; (2) explicit run instructions that include the word 'python' and the exact script filename ending in .py (use the same filename everywhere); and (3) a quiz section plus a clearly labeled 'Answer Key' section. Ensure the lesson examples reference the same .py filename used in the run instructions.

bundle_delivery.instruction: Replace the placeholder deliverable with a complete markdown package containing: (1) a full lesson in Markdown; (2) explicit run instructions that include the word 'python' and the exact script filename ending in .py (use the same filename everywhere); and (3) a quiz section plus a clearly labeled 'Answer Key' section. Ensure the lesson examples reference the same .py filename used in the run instructions.
